title: Wiring a 13A plug correctly
subtitle: Strip flex, set conductors to the right terminals, and match fuse rating to the appliance
excerpt: How to wire a UK 13A plug onto a length of flex: strip the outer and inner insulation cleanly, dress the conductors to the right terminals, hold the cord with the strain relief, and pick the correct fuse for the appliance rating.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. BS 1363 plug standard reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: The brown wire in modern flex, carrying mains voltage. Connects to the fused right-hand terminal of a UK 13A plug (looking at the back with the cord entry at the bottom).
The blue wire in modern flex, returning current at near-earth potential. Connects to the left-hand terminal of a UK 13A plug (looking at the back with the cord entry at the bottom).
The green-and-yellow striped wire in modern flex, connecting the appliance metal case to earth. Connects to the top terminal (the longer pin) of a UK 13A plug.

content.text: A UK 13A plug carries the line, neutral, and earth conductors of an appliance flex to the socket. Wired well, it gives years of service. Wired wrong, it shocks the user or trips the circuit. The job has five short steps and one decision: which fuse goes in the holder.
When to use this technique

Most modern appliances come with a moulded plug that cannot be opened. This technique is for older equipment with a screw-on plug, or for shortening or replacing a damaged flex on lighting and small appliances. New plug tops are sold at any electrical merchant for under a pound.
What you need

A new BS 1363 plug top with the right fuse. A 3A fuse for appliances rated up to 700 W (lamps, radios, chargers). A 13A fuse for appliances rated above 700 W (kettles, irons, heaters). The fuse rating is on the appliance label. Match the fuse to the appliance, not the plug.
Step 1, strip the outer sheath

Measure 50 mm of outer sheath at the cord end. Make a shallow circular cut with the stanley knife. Bend the flex back and forth at the cut until the sheath splits. Pull the sheath off the inner conductors. Do not cut into the inner conductor insulation. If a conductor is nicked, cut back and start the strip again 10 mm further along.
Step 2, strip each conductor

Use the wire strippers to remove 8 mm of insulation from each of the three conductors. The strippers have notches sized for common flex (0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5 mm squared). Pick the notch that just nips the insulation without cutting the copper. A clean strip leaves the copper bright and untwisted.
Step 3, dress the conductors to the terminals
Open the plug by undoing the centre screw and lifting the back off. The terminals are marked L (line), N (neutral), and E (earth). The
brown line conductor
goes to the fused right-hand terminal. The
blue neutral conductor
goes to the left-hand terminal. The
green-and-yellow earth conductor
goes to the top terminal. Cut each conductor a few millimetres long so it reaches its terminal without strain. Twist the bare copper ends so no stray strands stick out.
Step 4, fix into the terminals

Loosen each terminal screw. Push the bare end of each conductor fully into its terminal. Tighten the screw firmly. Tug each conductor by hand to check it is gripped. A loose conductor at a terminal heats up under load and burns out the plug.
Step 5, set the strain relief and fuse

Tighten the cord clamp at the base of the plug down onto the outer sheath of the flex, not onto the inner conductors. The clamp takes the strain when the plug is pulled out of the socket. Drop the correct fuse into the fuse holder. Refit the back of the plug and tighten the centre screw.
What it looks like when it is right

The closed plug sits flat against the appliance flex with no loose conductor visible at the cord entry. The cord clamp grips the outer sheath only, with the inner conductors fully shrouded. Plugged into a socket tester, all three indicator LEDs show correct polarity.
Common mistakes

Wrong fuse for the appliance. A 13A fuse in a lamp plug allows fault current up to 13A before tripping, well above the 3A flex rating. The flex melts before the fuse blows. Always match the fuse to the appliance label.
Strain relief clamping the inner conductors instead of the outer sheath. The conductors are squeezed against each other and the insulation cracks under load. Always clamp the outer sheath.
Stray copper strands at a terminal. A single stray strand reaching across to the next terminal short-circuits the plug. Always twist the bare end firmly and check no strands sit outside the terminal.

Old colour code mixed with new. Flex made before 2004 uses red for line, black for neutral, green for earth. Modern flex uses brown for line, blue for neutral, green and yellow for earth. On an old flex, match red to brown (line), black to blue (neutral), green to green and yellow (earth).
